coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly synthesis of CQDs from Pistachio shells: Versatile applications in anti-counterfeiting, flexible films, latent fingerprints and potential anti-cancer activity

coredata.dc:description: The synthesis of eco-friendly carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) from Pistachio shells is a straightforward process with versatile applications. These PCQDs, derived from non-toxic sources, are generated through a hydrothermal method and have garnered significant attention due to their outstanding properties. While there are many advanced CQDs available, the full potential of PCQDs remains untapped. These PCQDs, with their exceptional optical characteristics, serve as highly sensitive nano-probes for fluorometrically measuring Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence (FL) intensity of these nano-probes shows a linear correlation with Cu2+ ion concentration in the range of 0–4 µM, boasting a low detection limit of around 0.351 µM. This sensitivity is achieved through resonance energy and electron transfer-induced FL quenching. Importantly, carbon dots from Pistachio shells can be used as ink-free patterned substrates, making them ideal for printing and non-destructive collection and detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs). The LFPs can be developed using a simple powder dusting method with PCQDs and are visible under 365 nm UV light. This technique allows for lifting and identifying long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing any damage. Additionally, extensive research has been conducted on various pore properties such as size, shape, number, position, type, and inter-distance. Mathematical modelling implemented in Python-based software was used to investigate single fingerprints and two overlapped fingerprints. Furthermore, a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) film is fabricated using PCQDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), offering effective AC capabilities through their green emission under 365 nm UV light. Notably, these PCQDs exhibit sustainability, exceptional flexibility, and foldability, expanding their potential applications in flexible display devices, dermatoglyphics and data encryption. The overall results clearly demonstrate that PCQDs are a highly efficient luminescent platform with a wide range of applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of eco-friendly carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) from Pistachio shells is a straightforward process with versatile applications. These PCQDs, derived from non-toxic sources, are generated through a hydrothermal method and have garnered significant attention due to their outstanding properties. While there are many advanced CQDs available, the full potential of PCQDs remains untapped. These PCQDs, with their exceptional optical characteristics, serve as highly sensitive nano-probes for fluorometrically measuring Cu2+ ions. The fluorescence (FL) intensity of these nano-probes shows a linear correlation with Cu2+ ion concentration in the range of 0–4 µM, boasting a low detection limit of around 0.351 µM. This sensitivity is achieved through resonance energy and electron transfer-induced FL quenching. Importantly, carbon dots from Pistachio shells can be used as ink-free patterned substrates, making them ideal for printing and non-destructive collection and detection of latent fingerprints (LFPs). The LFPs can be developed using a simple powder dusting method with PCQDs and are visible under 365 nm UV light. This technique allows for lifting and identifying long-term exposed LFPs from various surfaces without causing any damage. Additionally, extensive research has been conducted on various pore properties such as size, shape, number, position, type, and inter-distance. Mathematical modelling implemented in Python-based software was used to investigate single fingerprints and two overlapped fingerprints. Furthermore, a transparent anti-counterfeiting (AC) film is fabricated using PCQDs and polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), offering effective AC capabilities through their green emission under 365 nm UV light. Notably, these PCQDs exhibit sustainability, exceptional flexibility, and foldability, expanding their potential applications in flexible display devices, dermatoglyphics and data encryption. The overall results clearly demonstrate that PCQDs are a highly efficient luminescent platform with a wide range of applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The security of critical information has garnered considerable attention in modern society, leading to extensive research endeavors aimed at developing advanced materials for storing and transmitting confidential data [1]. In this context, materials with exceptional fluorescence properties, including tunable fluorescence emission and excellent fluorescence stability, are highly sought after. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), distinguished by their photoluminescence (PL) properties, have garnered significant attention and found diverse applications across various fields [2,3]. Their exceptional attributes, such as controllable photoluminescence, remarkable aqueous solubility, excellent biological safety, facile synthesis, and surface modification capabilities, render them well-suited for endeavors ranging from information encryption and biosensing to drug delivery and photocatalysis [4-8].

Water treatment and metal ion sensing in aquatic environments stand out as significant applications for CDs. For instance, the detection of Cu2+ ions in water are crucial, particularly given the potential for Cu2+ contamination in drinking water due to corroded piping systems. Elevated levels of dissolved Cu2+ not only affect the taste of drinking water but can also pose serious health risks, including vomiting, diarrhea, stomach cramps, and liver damage, if they exceed safety standards [9,10]. Consequently, while carbon dots (CDs) have found extensive application as probes for fluorescence detection of Cu2+, their effectiveness has been hindered by poor stability and low fluorescent quantum yield. This limitation severely restricts their utility in diverse fields, notably in advanced information encryption and biological imaging.

Counterfeiting or forgery represents a pervasive problem with wide-ranging repercussions for both individuals and society. It undermines public trust and safety by flooding markets with illicit goods, documents, and currency, thereby posing significant economic and health risks [11-13]. Particularly concerning is the proliferation of counterfeit medicines in the pharmaceutical market, where substandard products can evade quality control measures, endangering public health and safety. Counterfeiting poses severe environmental, health, economic, and social consequences, prompting a global call for innovative and highly secure anti-counterfeiting solutions [14-16].

Researchers worldwide are challenged to devise materials capable of generating non-forgeable security codes and markers, thereby addressing the escalating threat of forgeries. This pursuit underscores the urgent need to safeguard consumers and businesses, ensuring a safe and sustainable environment for all. In recent years, an array of anti-counterfeiting, verification, and authentication technologies have emerged to protect documents and products, including signature-DNA-marker packaging materials, magnetic response plasmonic security labels, anti-theft tags, RFID (Radio-Frequency Identification) based smart labels, and luminescence printing. Nevertheless, certain approaches are susceptible to cloning due to their predictable and deterministic encoding mechanisms [17]. Among these various strategies, luminescence printing stands out as a popular method in the fight against counterfeiting due to its numerous advantages, including the simple design of luminescent materials, ease of handling, and high throughput. Selecting the right materials is paramount for creating durable and tamper-proof security printing features. These materials must be easy to synthesize with high yields, environmentally friendly, stable over time, cost-effective, and most importantly, possess distinguishing yet challenging-to-replicate features. Luminescent materials have emerged as leaders in the anti-counterfeiting realm, finding applications in optical data recording, storage, and security. Their unique optical properties make them exceptionally difficult to replicate [18].

Hence, the integration of highly luminescent carbon dots into suitable solid-state matrices presents an appealing opportunity to harness their properties for practical device applications. However, stabilizing them in solid materials like polymers, ceramics, and inorganic oxides poses a significant challenge, as fluorescence quenching pathways may arise from CD aggregation or chemical interactions on the CD's external surface. Numerous studies have demonstrated the successful integration of carbon dots into polymer matrices like polymethylmethacrylate (PMMA), polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), or gel glass [19,20]. In these cases, the CDs embedded in solid polymeric films exhibited stable photoluminescence properties. From a green and sustainable development standpoint, opting for PVA is highly significant due to its eco-friendly nature, biodegradability, and excellent light transmittance. Thus, the composite film exhibits a combination of attributes including high transparency, flexibility, foldability, scalability, and efficiency, making it a promising alternative in the field of anti-counterfeiting, particularly as a substitute for petroleum-based materials.

For more than a century, law enforcement agencies worldwide have recognized the immense value of fingerprint evidence for individual identification and forensic analysis. Enhancing the visibility of latent fingerprints or other human traces remains a top priority in criminal investigations, aiding in the collection of crucial physical evidence at crime scenes [24,25]. Consequently, forensic scientists have developed a variety of approaches for enhancing latent fingerprints, including regular powder, fluorescent powder, cyanoacrylate fuming, ninhydrin method, 1,8-diazafluoren-9-one method, and small particle reagent method. Among these methods, powder dusting stands out as the most pervasive worldwide due to its portability, high efficiency, and ease of operation. While rare earth-based powders, including down-conversion, up-conversion, and long afterglow nanophosphors, have been extensively utilized in forensic science for several years, the powder dusting method has faced criticism due to the issue of dust blowing, posing potential health hazards to users. Thus, magnetic powders, which allow for controlled powder application using magnetic brushes, are commonly regarded as one of the most effective solutions to mitigate or reduce the risk of dust blowing. However, traditional magnetic powders used for enhancing latent fingerprints often lack fluorescence, which diminishes the ridge signal and reduces fingerprint contrast, particularly on multi-colored substrates or textured surfaces. Moreover, commercial fluorescent powders typically comprise hybrid formulations, often incorporating organic dyes as luminescent components along with other conventional powders to ensure optimal flowability during powder brushing. As a result, the significant variation in particle shape and size among hybrid powders may impede the adhesion of powders to fingerprint residues, ultimately reducing the sensitivity and selectivity of latent fingerprint detection [26,27]. Hence, there is an urgent need for the development of a novel powder with multifunctionality, low toxicity, and high effectiveness in forensic science. Table 1 shows the synthesis of carbon dots and its diverse applications [28–40]. Comparative study of LFP detection with different carbon sources are listed in Table 2 [41–50].

This study introduces a solvent-free, eco-friendly approach for the synthesis of PCQDs through one step hydrothermal route. These PCQDs are sourced from Pistachio shells, obtained from a single, readily available origin, and prepared under precisely controlled conditions. Therefore, in this work we anticipate the obtained high-efficient green emitting PCQDs possess remarkable application possibilities on the multifunctional platforms involving anti-counterfeiting, flexible displays, latent fingerprint, metal ion sensing and biological applications.

Deionized water (DI) was purified using a Milli-Q Plus system from Millipore to provide the high-purity (UP) water employed in all the experiments. Chemicals such as Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), Magnesium chloride (MgCl2), Potassium chloride (KCl), Copper(II) chloride (CuCl2), Manganese(II) chloride (MnCl2), Cobalt(II) chloride (CoCl2), Ferric Chloride (FeCl3), Zinc chloride (ZnCl2), Nickel(II) chloride (NiCl2), Silicon tetra-chloride (SiCl4), Cyclohexane (C6H12), Acetonitrile (C2H3N), Ethanol (C2H5OH), Methanol (CH3OH), Diethylene glycol (C4H10O3), Triethylamine (C6H15N), Hexane (C₆H₁₄), Toluene (C₆H₅CH₃), and Propylene glycol (C3H8O2) were all sourced from Sigma Aldrich Co., Ltd. India. The Pistachio shell was bought at a nearby grocery store in Tumkur province, Indian. Metal ions were dissolved in water using their sulphate or chloride salts. Every chemical used in the studies was analytical grade and didn't require any additional purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PCQDs from Pistachio Shell
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize PCQDs, a hydrothermal method was employed. Initially, Pistachio shells were mechanically crushed using a mortar and pestle. Subsequently, 10 gm of the resulting Pistachio shell powder were combined with 50 mL of deionized (DI) water in a beaker and stirred for several min. After that, the suspension was placed in a stainless-steel autoclave with a Teflon lining, and it was heated to 200 °C for 1 hr. The autoclave was left to cool naturally to ambient temperature after the synthesis period. A mixture of dark brown powder and a brown solution was seen when the Teflon container was opened. To remove larger insoluble particles, the brown solution was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for a period of 5 min. The obtained supernatants of the respective samples containing PCQDs have further purified by filtering through 0.22 μm filter membrane. The solution was subsequently placed within a dialysis bag (100–500 Da, Solar bio). The sealed dialysis bag was immersed in water and subjected to gentle magnetic stirring for 24 hrs and water is replaced every 6 hrs. The dialysis was carried out to obtain PCQDs. The obtained PCQDs have further lyophilized to remove the moisture content and obtained powder stored at room temperature for further characterization and latent fingerprint visualization. Fig. 1a provides a schematic representation of the PCQDs synthesis process from Pistachio shells and showcases the appearance of the PCQDs solution under both ambient light and UV-light. The PCQDs solution showed a brown colour and luminescent a striking green when subjected to 365 nm UV light.

In this procedure, 30 mL of Deionised (DI) water was used to dissolve 2 gm of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), which has an average molecular weight of 125,000 g mol−1. This solution was attained by cooling it to room temperature (RT) and stirring it with a magnetic stirrer at 90 °C until it became clear. Then, to guarantee complete mixing, the necessary volume of PVA solution was added to the previously made PCQDs and gently stirred using magnetic stirrer at RT for ∼30 min. The resulting solution, which contained the PCQDs and PVA mixture, was utilized as AC ink for information encryption. The schematic representation of development of AC labels is shown in Fig. 1b.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Latent fingerprint enhancement and Gray analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The latent fingerprints (LFPs) were obtained from a 25-year-old male volunteer by gently rubbing his fingers on his nose and forehead before touching various surfaces. Different substrates including paper, OHP sheet, and a steel lock were selected for depositing the LFPs. The powder dusting method, aided by a magnetic brush, was employed to develop the latent fingerprints using PCQDs. The fingerprint images were inspected under 365 nm irradiation and then captured using a digital camera (Canon EOS 7D Mark III DSLR). The process of developing latent fingerprints is depicted in Fig. 1 c. The colour fingerprint image underwent conversion to grayscale to ensure the ridge had a higher grayscale value than the furrow. When required, a negative digital image transformation was applied. Following this, the grayscale fingerprint image was loaded into ImageJ software, where the straight-line tool was used to intersect the ridges and furrows. The lines intersected the ridges perpendicularly, with a focus on ensuring a minimum of ten crossings for both ridges and furrows. Subsequently, the Plot Profile tool within the Analyses menu was employed to generate the Gray curves between the intersecting papillary ridges and furrows. In this representation, the crest indicated the ridge, while the trough indicated the small furrow.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of fluorescent PCQDs @PVA composite Films
ce:section.ce:para: The following procedures were taken in order to prepare the AC film: In 20 mL of DI water, 5 gm of PVA was first dissolved. Using a magnetic stirrer, the dissolution procedure was carried out at 80 °C until a clear solution was produced. Afterward, the solution was allowed to cool to RT. Subsequently, the appropriate amount of the PVA solution was combined with 1 mL of a 1 mg mL−1 PCQDs solution under constant stirring with a magnetic stirrer at RT for 30 min to ensure thorough mixing. The mixture was then placed onto a sanitized glass slide and baked to dry it out. The film was gently removed from the glass substrate when it had dried, creating a freestanding film. Preparation of fluorescent PCQDs@PVA composite films is show in Fig. 1d.

The PCQDs solution, containing 0.50 mg mL−1 of mass, was chosen for examining its selectivity with respect to different metal ions at RT. Simultaneously, ten stock solutions (5 mM each) were individually prepared, containing metal ions such as Cd2+, Mg2+, K+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, and Si2+ from their respective salts. Typically, 3 mL of the PCQDs solution was gently shaken with 1 mL of each metal salt solution. All experiments were conducted consistently, and the pertinent FL spectra were recorded after allowing the reactions to proceed for 10 min at RT. To assess the sensitivity of PCQDs toward Cu2+, the same procedure was followed, upon adding various concentrations of Cu2+ (1 mL) to a solution of 3 mL of PCQDs (0.50 mg mL−1). The Cu2+ values varied between 0 and 4 mM. The excitation wavelength was adjusted at 414 nm.

The FL quantum yield (Φ) for PCQDs was determined by employing an aqueous solution of quinine sulphate in a 0.1 M H2SO4 solution, as outlined in Eq.1 [51].

Meanwhile, φX and φR represent the QY values for the synthesized PCQDs and quinine sulphate (54% at 365 nm), respectively. ηC and ηR denote the corresponding refractive indices for water and the H2SO4 solution. IC and IR stand for the integrated emission area for PCQDs and the reference dye. In this case, η is equal to 1, as the aqueous system was employed. AC and AR refer to the absorbance values for PCQDs and the reference dye, respectively. To minimize the impact of the inner filter effect, efforts were made to maintain the absorbance values below 0.10 at the respective excitation wavelength.

The free radical scavenging capacity of PCQDs was assessed following the methodology outlined by Krishnappa et al., [52] employing the stable 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH) radical. In brief, the reaction mixture consisted of 0.1 mM DPPH and varying concentrations of PCQDs (50, 100, 150, 200, and 250 µg mL−1) in 1 mL of methanol, and the mixture was incubated for ∼30 min at RT. Subsequently, at 517 nm, the absorbance was recorded, with ascorbic acid serving as the standard.

of

× 100

The HCT-116 cell line (Human colorectal adenocarcinoma) was procured from NCCS, Pune, India. These cells were cultured in DMEM with high glucose medium, enriched with 10% FBS and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic solution. Culturing was maintained in a controlled environment with 5% CO2, 18–20% O2 at 37 °C in a CO2 incubator, with sub-culturing performed every two days. The cells used for the current study were at passage number 33.

To assess the impact of PCQDs on HTC-116 cells, we followed the methodology outlined by Isabelle Fakhoury et al. [53]. Various concentrations of PCQDs (12.5, 25, 50, 100, and 200 µg) and 50 µM 5-fluorouracil were dissolved in DMSO. A 200 µL suspension of HTC-116 cells (20,000 cells/well) in DMSO was seeded in a 96-well plate and incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Following incubation, the media was eliminated, and DMSO was used to wash the cells. Various concentrations of PCQDs, DMSO (without PCQDs) as a negative control, and 50 µM 5-fluorouracil as a positive control were added and incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 atmosphere. Next, the medium was aspirated, and an MTT solution (0.5 mg mL−1) was added in equal volume, followed by incubation in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 hrs. Subsequently, the medium was removed, and 100 μL of DMSO was introduced. The culture plates were gently agitated on a gyratory shaker for 30 min to ensure complete solubilization of the formed purple crystals. The enzymatic reduction of the yellow tetrazolium dye was measured at 570 nm using a Multi-mode Micro-plate Reader.

We employed a flow cytometry-based approach to quantitatively assess the cellular uptake of FITC-conjugated PCQDs, following the methodology described by Maya Khater et al. [54]. HTC-116 cells were seeded in 6-well plates (2×105 cells/2 mL) and incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C under a CO2 atmosphere. Subsequently, the cells were treated with FITC-PCQDs and incubated for 6 hrs in a dark environment. Afterward, the medium was aspirated, and the cells were gently washed with PBS. To detach the cells, 500 μL of trypsin-EDTA was added and incubated at 37 °C for 3–4 min. The harvested cells were analysed via flow cytometry, employing a 488 nm laser for excitation and detecting FL at 535 nm.

To quantify HTC-116 cell apoptosis, we followed the protocol as described by Zohreh Efati et al. [55]. 200 µL of HTC-116 cell suspensions were seeded in 6 well plates with 20,000 cells/well using DMSO, and the plates were then incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C with 5% CO2 in the air. After the incubation period, the media was taken out and the cells were carefully treated a DMSO wash. Subsequently, we introduced 57 µg mL−1 of PCQDs, using DMSO as a negative control (without PCQDs), and 50 µM 5-FLU as a positive control, to the cells. This mix was incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C under a 5% CO2 atmosphere. After treating the cells with a PBS wash, 500 μL of trypsin-EDTA was added, and the cells were incubated for three to four min at 37 °C. Afterward, the cells were centrifuged (300 x g and 25 °C for 3 min), and they were washed again with PBS. We proceeded to assess cell apoptosis by adding 5 μL of FITC Annex in V in 100 μL of Annexin V binding buffer, followed by 5 min incubation at RT. After this, 5 μL of Propidium Iodide (PI) was added, along with 400 μL of 1X Annexin Binding Buffer. Finally, HTC-116 cell apoptosis was analysed using a flow cytometer (BD FACS Caliber, BD Bio-sciences, CA, USA).

To measure ROS levels, we followed the protocol outlined by Simon Ting et al. [56]. In brief, HTC-116 cells were seeded in 6 well plates at a density of 0.5×106 cells in 2 mL of medium and incubated for 24 hrs at 37 °C. After incubation, the cells were washed with 1 mL of PBS. Subsequently, we treated the cells with 57 µg/mL of PCQDs and 50 µM mL−1 of 5-fluorouracil for 24 hrs. Following this treatment, the medium was removed, and the cells were washed with PBS. We added 500 μL of trypsin-EDTA and incubated the cells at 37 °C for 3–4 min. The cells were then centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 6 min, and the supernatant was discarded. The cells were re-suspended in 1 mL of PBS and incubated with 4 mM DCFH-DA for 30 min at 37 °C. Finally, we measured intra-cellular FL levels using a flow cytometer with an excitation wavelength (λexci= 488 nm) and an emission wavelength (λemi =525 nm).

The antimicrobial activity of the sample against three test organisms, Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC 1430), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 424), and Candida albicans (MTCC 227), was assessed using the PCQDs well diffusion assay. Brain Heart Infusion PCQDs and Luria Bertani PCQDs served as the diffusion media for bacteria and fungi, respectively. In each solidified PCQDs plate, four wells were created by using a sterile cork borer. We spread-plated 0.1 mL of Staphylococcus aureus, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and Candida albicans on the PCQDs plates. Inside the wells of each PCQDs plate, we loaded different concentrations of PCQDs (10, 25, and 50 µg mL−1) and positive controls (ampicillin for bacteria and amphotericin for fungi). The plates were then incubated at 37 °C for 24 hrs for bacterial plates and at room temperature for 3–5 days for fungal plates. To assess the antimicrobial effect, we measured the diameter of the zone of inhibition to the nearest millimeter (mm) using a vernier callipers.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with the HITACHI LTD H-7500 and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) with the JEOL JEM-2100 were used to analyse the morphology of PCQDs. PCQDs were subjected to X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) investigation using a monochromatic Al Kα radiation source and a PHI 5000 Versa-probe spectrometer. Using a Perkin-Elmer spectrophotometer, the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra of Pistachio shell and PCQDs are determined. A JASCO V-670 spectrophotometer was used to record the UV–VIS spectra of PCQDs. The PL and PLE of PCQDs are evaluated at ambient temperature using a Horiba (Jobin Yvon) spectrofluorometer, which utilizes a 450 W xenon lamp for excitation, set at a slit width of 2 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analyses are performed using a Shimadzu 7000 powder X-ray diffractometer, employing Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å.

The crystallinity of the synthesized PCQDs was confirmed via X-ray diffraction (XRD), as illustrated in Fig. 2a. The broad diffraction peak that was produced and centered at 22.52° corresponds to the (002) plane. This indicates that the hexagonal graphite framework of PCQDs has inter-layer packing of disordered carbon atoms, which results in a turbostratic structure [57,58]. This broad peak also indicates the amorphous nature of the formed particles. Furthermore, a weak peak along the (100) plane at 44.54 ° indicates a degree of graphitization. It was found that the inter-layer distance (d-spacing) is 0.37 nm for (002) plane, while 0.21 nm for (100) plane. Raman analysis further validates the graphitic nature of PCQDs, displaying two broad peaks at 1352 and 1546 cm−1, corresponding to the D and G bands, respectively [59] (Fig. 2b). The G band is indicative of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a hexagonal lattice, whereas, the D band represents disorder in the graphite lattice and is linked to the existence of sp3 defects [60]. For PCQDs, the integrated intensity ratio (ID/IG) of the D and G bands is around 0.66, suggesting a slightly disordered graphite-like structure and offering information on the level of functionalization (sp3/sp2 carbon ratio) and disorder [61]. To characterize the morphologies of PCQDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed. The PCQDs are uniformly dispersed, displaying spherical particles with an average diameter of approximately 4 nm (Fig. 2c). The graphite framework's (100) lattice planes are responsible for the uniform size distribution and strong crystallinity, as confirmed by an HR-TEM image [62] (Fig. 2d). The lattice spacing is estimated to be 0.22 nm. According to the statistic particle size distribution histograms shown in Fig. 2e, these PCQDs have an average size of ∼4 nm and a narrow size distribution from 4 to 5 nm is agreement with the TEM image. In Fig. 2f, the scanning electron microscope (SEM) image of PCQDs clearly reveals uniform spherical particles without agglomeration.

Employing energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX), the elemental composition of the produced PCQDs was ascertained (Fig. 2g). The observations indicate high concentrations of carbon (48.45 wt%), nitrogen (10.39 wt%), and oxygen (41.16 wt%), indicating that the surface of the produced PCQDs contains a variety of functionalization groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, carbonyl and amine groups. The greater oxygen groups seen in the EDX spectra of PCQDs are probably due to the presence of O groups in the functional groups. Fig. 2h presents the photoluminescence (PL) emission spectrum of PCQDs, excited at a wavelength of 414 nm. An intense peak is observed at a wavelength of 529 nm. Additionally, the CIE coordinates, indicating colour, were estimated and found to be (0.300, 0.620) (inset of Fig. 2h).

Using conventional XPS technique, the composition of the elements and surface functional groups of the PCQDs were determined. The full XPS spectrum, depicted in Fig. 3a, reveals two prominent peaks at approximately 532 and 284 eV, as well as a weaker peak at around 401 eV. In the PCQDs, oxygen-rich functional groups and species containing nitrogen are confirmed by these, which correspond to the O 1 s, C 1 s, and N 1 s, on the respectively. The high-resolution C 1 s signal spectrum (Fig. 3b) exhibits four peaks at approximately 283.7, 284.3, 284.8, and 288.3 eV, representing the CC, C-C, C-O/C-N, and CO/CN groups, respectively [63–65]. Furthermore, the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum ( Fig. 3c) shows distinct nitrogen species (graphitic N at 401 eV), providing strong evidence for the synthesis of PCQDs [66]. The O1s spectrum ( Fig. 3d) was deconvoluted into four fitted peaks at approximately 532.2 eV, 532.3, 532.5, and 533.5 eV. These findings unequivocally verify that PCQDs surface contains O–C–O, CO, C–O/C–OH/C–O–C, and OC–O groups [67]. The resultant PCQDs are extremely compatible with water because of this special property. In fact, even after remaining at ambient temperature for 6 months, the supernatants solution is homogeneous and shows no discernible precipitation.

The FT-IR spectra of a pure Pistachio shell is shown in Fig. 3e. The spectrum displays multiple peaks indicating C-H stretching: 1466 (CH aliphatic bending groups), 1374 (CH3 in proteins), 3001 (C-H stretching alkene groups), 2921 and 2816 (CH or CH2 stretching aliphatic groups), and 756 cm−1 (CH CH). A peak for O-H stretching is located at 3342 cm−1. There are also notable peaks at 1722, 1684, 1632, 1529, 1215, and 1145 cm−1, which are related to the vibrations of the CO, NH3, CC, amid II, stretching of the C-N, stretching of the C-O-C, and the C-O ring in carbohydrates, respectively. The presence of various nitrogen and oxygen functional groups and linkages in PCQDs as shown in Fig. 3f. The presence of -OH, -C-H, CO, C-N and N-H, was clearly indicated by stretching frequencies at 3368, 2946, 1612, 1192 and 855 cm−1, respectively. The stretching vibration of CH shown by the peak at 1494 cm−1, while C–NH–C and C–N/C–O groups have a peak at 1387 cm−1, 1093 cm−1 and 1005 cm−1 respectively [68–70]. Because of the presence of these hydrophilic functional groups, the PCQDs that are produced are exceedingly water soluble.

Fig. 4a illustrates how UV–Vis absorption and FL spectroscopy were used to investigate the optical characteristics of the PCQDs. A broad optical absorption peak is visible in the UV–Vis spectrum, mainly at 278 nm in the UV region. The π-π* and n-π* electron transitions in conjugated carbonyl groups (CC/CO) may be the cause of this peak [71]. The PCQDs show excellent emission and excitation wavelengths (λemi=529 nm and λexci 414 nm), in terms of PL spectra. Digital images of PCQD solutions under day time irradiation (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) are shown in the insets of Fig. 4a. Remarkably the PCQDs solution exhibits strong green FL and appears translucent, clear, and pale yellow in day light. Fig. 4b displays the emission spectra of PCQDs stimulated at various wavelengths (345–455 nm). The as-synthesized PCQDs appear to exhibit the typical excitation dependent PL behaviour, with 365 nm UV light eliciting the greatest emission peak. PL was recorded for shorter excited wavelengths (255 – 335 nm) are shown in Figure S1. However, for 345–455 nm excitation clearly indicate that the highest emission (529 nm) was noticed at 414 nm excitation wavelength, which are consistent with the peak in the excitation and absorption spectra. When the excitation wavelength increases from 345 to 455 nm, the emission peak moves to longer wavelengths, from 529 to 545 nm. The intricate nature of many surface states in PCQDs and anti-Stokes PL can be used to explain the occurrence [72], and it is consistent with earlier findings on fluorescent PCQDs [73].

A detailed analysis of the emission characteristics of carbon dots diluted in pH buffer solutions is presented in Fig. 4c, which shows the FL intensities vs pH. The degree of FL increased in extremely acidic conditions (pH 3–5) as the pH increased. Furthermore, when the pH was further raised (within the range of 5–11), the emission intensity of PCQDs remained relatively stable. These carbon dots exhibited pH dependent characteristics primarily in acidic environments, with minimal changes observed in basic conditions [74]. The presence of amines on the surface played a significant role as Lewis bases. When these amines and acids combined to generate ammonium salts, the surface state of PCQDs changed, which in turn caused the FL to be quenched in very acidic conditions. Additionally, research was done on the stability of PCQDs in different NaCl concentrations, revealing that FL intensity remained nearly constant across the range of 0–2 molar concentrations (Fig. 4d). Because of the functional groups on their surface, carbon dots exhibit extraordinary stability in aqueous solutions. According to these results, carbon dots that have been manufactured may find use in pH monitoring and high-ionic concentration settings.

The synthesized PCQDs have the potential to be excellent candidates for sensing applications because of their remarkable photoluminescent capabilities, great chemical stability, photo-stability, superior water solubility, and abundance of surface functional groups. Interestingly, the coordination effect can lead to the complexes or chelates between metal ions and the oxygen functional groups on the PCQD surface [75–77]. Thus, a lot of research was done to determine whether employing PCQDs as probes for metal ion detection would be feasible. To assess the selectivity of the sensing system, maximum PL intensities of PCQDs solutions were observed with independent additions of respective metal ions (5 mM) under the same conditions. These metal ions included K+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, and Si2+. The PL peak properties of PCQDs were somewhat altered by the addition of each metal ion, as Fig. 4e illustrates.

The strongest ability to extinguish PCQD FL was shown by Cu2+ among these metal ions. One explanation for the quenching of FL of PCQDs by Cu2+ was the non-luminous complex that formed between Cu2+ and the groups on the PCQD surfaces that contained oxygen or nitrogen. An electron transfer pathway from PCQDs to Cu2+ d orbitals was non-radiative and involved Cu2+, a universal electron acceptor. Fig. 4f displayed the (F/F0) bar diagram with a 5 μM concentration of each metal ion. The PL intensities in the presence and absence of metal ions were represented by F and F0, respectively, upon excitation by UV light (365 nm).

Surprisingly, other metal ions had very little effect on PCQDs PL intensity, with the exception of Cu2+ (> 58%) and Si2+ (∼62%). This implies that Cu2+ detection was not substantially affected by the majority of metal ions. This discriminative impact was probably caused by Cu2+ greater affinity and rapid chelation process with the O- (or N-) containing groups on PCQDs surface as compared to other metal ions [78]. These findings show that the PCQDs for the detection of Cu2+ in aqueous solutions have a steady response and great selectivity. The impact of several potentially interfering metal ions (each at a 100 M concentration), such as Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Sn2+, and Zn2+, on the fluorescent response of the PCQDs sensing probe was examined, in addition to Cu2+ at a concentration of 100 M, as shown in Fig. 4g, in order to further evaluate the selectivity of the PCQDs towards Cu2+ ions. The results demonstrated that the presence of interfering metal ions did not significantly affect the detection of Cu2+ ions.

where F0: FL intensity of PCQDs in the absence of Cu2+, F: FL intensity of PCQDs in the presence of Cu2+. Using the Stern-Volmer equation F0/F=1 + Ksv[Cu2+], the Ksv (Stern-Volmer quenching constant) was calculated as 0.0181 M−1. With the use of the IUPAC three times standard deviation rule, the limit of detection (LOD) of 0.351 μM was determined simultaneously (LOD = 3σ/s, where s is the slope of the linear calibration curve and σ is the standard deviation of the blank signals). The estimated LOD is in agreement with findings from earlier study [79]. Comparison of CDs based sensors for Cu2+ detection is tabulated in Table 3 [80–85]. Notably, our PCQDs LOD is less than the highest permitted concentration of Cu2+ in drinking water (3 ppm) according to the Indian standard, highlighting their excellent potential for Cu2+ detection in real-world applications.

Further investigation was conducted on the photo-stability of PCQDs, which is a crucial property for real-world applications. Fig. 4j shows the PCQDs solution FL emission spectra at different storage times. Significantly, from the first synthesis to the 90-day storage period, the FL intensities of PCQDs stayed almost constant, demonstrating their long-term stability. The photo-stability of PCQDs was examined before and after exposure to 365 nm light for 90 min using a high-intensity (6 mW cm−2) UV lamp. The results are displayed in Fig. 4k. Remarkably, irradiated PCQDs exhibited only slight differences in FL intensity compared to non-irradiated PCQDs, confirming the excellent photo-stability of PCQDs [86]. Furthermore, the quinine sulfate and PCQDs solutions were employed to estimate the QY by utilizing UV-Vis absorption and PL emission spectra, as shown in Figs. 4l and 4m. The estimated QY of the PCQDs was found to be 15%.

Fig. 5a demonstrates the intriguing quenching and re-quenching behaviour of PCQDs. The introduction of 15 M Cd2+ successfully re-quenches the FL of PCQDs. Interestingly, the colour of the PCQDs solution changes from black to green, signifying the restoration of FL properties. This recovery of FL is attributed to the release of loosely bound Cu2+ ions from the surface of PCQDs, facilitated by the strong affinity between Cd2+ and Cu2+. The quenching and re-quenching mechanism of FL is employed for turn ON-OFF experiments, and the performance of the logic gate (LG) is also investigated [87]. A schematic of the FL ON-OFF experiment using PCQDs from different sources may be found in Fig. 5b. When Cu2+ and Cd2+ are present, PCQDs FL switching activity can be used to create several molecular logic gates that carry out algebraic Boolean logic operations. Cu2+ can be used as a single input signal to create a straightforward one input molecular logic gate NOT (Fig. 5c).With Cu2+ and Cd2+ assigned as inputs 1 and 2, respectively, the multiple input logic gate IMPLICATION can be realized. The values for Cu2+ and Cd2+ are 1 and 0, respectively, while their absence is 0. The output maximum FL was determined to be 1 and its quenched FL to be 0. Fig. 5d illustrates that significant quenching of the FL is only detected when Cu2+ is present and Cd2+ is absent, or when the input is (1, 0), resulting in an output of 0. The result remains at 1 for all other inputs (0/0, 0/1, and 1/1). Fig. 5e shows the truth table, output FL intensity at 529 nm, and the logic symbol.

The solution behaviour of PCQDs was explored in 10 different solvents with distinct physico-chemical characteristics. A stable dispersion was obtained in water, cyclohexane, acetonitrile, ethanol, methanol, diethylene glycol, triethylamine, hexane, toluene, and 1,2-propanediol after 1 hr of sonication, indicating their suitability as solvents for the PCQDs [88]. Furthermore, PCQDs exhibited good solubility and FL emission under 365 nm UV illumination ( Fig. 6a). A slight shift in the FL emission peak is noted, likely due to nanoparticle aggregation. Photographs of the PCQDs solutions under 365 nm illumination are presented in Fig. 6b.

The optical properties of the PCQDs were further explored through a FL lifetime study. The FL decay curve of PCQDs was accurately fitted with a tri-exponential function as provided below [89];

In this analysis, the photoluminescence (PL) decay function Y(t) exhibits time-dependent characteristics, featuring three distinct decay lifetimes denoted as T1, T2, and T3, each contributing to the PL decay function with fractional weights A1, A2, and A3, respectively. Fig. 6c illustrates the decay curve, and further details regarding these distinct decay regions are provided in the inset of Fig. 6c. The exponential behaviour exhibited here suggests the presence of three unique emissive sites on PCQDs. Specifically, T1 corresponds to the direct electron-hole recombination lifetime, while T2 and T3 represent longer lifetimes indicative of the extended relaxation of excitons to lower energy levels. The successful fitting of the tri-exponential function (Rtri 2 = 0.999, surpassing Rbi 2 = 0.998) to the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) decay curves indicates the existence of three distinct emission sites, each with average lifetimes of (2.483 ± 2.725), (2.484 ± 3.300), and (11.345 ± 1.876) ns. In contrast, the average lifetime of PCQDs is measured at 5.97 ns. These estimated values align well with previous literature findings [90].

The utility of PCQDs as an illumination material for AC purposes is demonstrated, showcasing its potential to securely encode and decode information when subjected to 365 nm UV light, thus offering a robust method for protecting sensitive data [91–94]. In practical applications, we employed a manual writing mode to generate fluorescent patterns on a variety of surfaces, encompassing cellulose paper, ceramic, OHP sheet, plastic, metal, and paper. In broad daylight, these patterns remained concealed, leaving no discernible traces. However, when illuminated with 365 nm UV light, vibrant green patterns emerged, underscoring the efficacy of PCQDs-based fluorescent ink for security applications ( Fig. 7a). To further assess the durability of AC patterns produced using PCQDs fluorescent ink, we conducted a 200 day aging test. Even after this prolonged period, the emission colour of the AC pattern remained unchanged ( Fig. 7b). This robust and consistent green emission underscores the environmental resilience of PCQDs-based fluorescent ink, affirming its suitability for encryption and decryption applications. These findings provide compelling evidence of the reliability and long-lasting performance of PCQDs fluorescent ink in data security applications [95–99]. To evaluate the stability of AC patterns under varying temperatures, patterns created on marble were exposed to temperatures ranging from 100 to 200 °C for 30 min (Fig. 7c). Impressively, the patterns retained their distinct and consistent appearance up to 180 °C. The photo-stability of the produced AC images using PCQDs ink is a critical aspect for practical applications. In Fig. 7d, we present the AC images after varying UV exposure times (5–30 min). Notably, the fluorescent intensities of AC images remained nearly unaltered up to 25 min of exposure, with only minimal fluctuations observed after 30 min of exposure [100–102].

Applications in the plastic film and optoelectronic sectors depend heavily on the solid-state FL of PCQDs. Unfortunately, particle aggregation commonly leads to FL quenching in the solid state, resulting in significantly diminished FL properties compared to those in solution [103]. One well-recognized and effective way to counteract this quenching is to dispersion PCQDs within a polymer matrix [104]. Because of its exceptional passivation capabilities to the surface of PCQDs and strong compatibility with them, PVA is frequently acknowledged as an appropriate matrix for PCQDs [105]. Taking these factors into account, PCQDs were added to the PVA matrix to generate a PVA@PCQDs film. Digital photos of the PVA@PCQDs films under UV light (365 nm) and visible light irradiation are shown in Fig. 8a, respectively. The resultant film shows remarkable transparency in visible light, as shown in the figure, suggesting good dispersion of PCQDs in the PVA matrix. In addition, when exposed to 365 nm UV light, the solid PVA@PCQDs film glows vividly green, mimicking the behaviour of PCQDs in solution. Fig. 8b reveals that the resulting AC film is optically transparent, boasting an overall transmittance of 85% and an optical haze of just 10% at 550 nm. In response to the rapid advancement of technology, counterfeit information and products have become increasingly prevalent. To address this issue and promote AC packaging with the aforementioned advantages, PCQDs are dispersed in a PVA medium to create a transparent AC film. Fig. 8c provide a schematic representation of a UV-triggered hidden pattern PVA film employing PCQDs security ink. This process offers a straightforward means of implementing an AC code based on a predefined pattern. Fig. 8d provides photographic views of the PVA@PCQDs films undergoing various folds. These images clearly demonstrate that the PVA@PCQDs film maintains its vivid green emission even during various forms of deformation, such as twisting, bending, and rolling, indicating stable optical characteristics across a range of mechanical deformations, highlighting their suitability for numerous applications in the realm of flexible electronics. This visual evidence confirms that, when subjected to UV light excitation at 365 nm, they emit a vivid green light, underscoring that PVA does not hinder the excellent performance of PCQDs in photoluminescence assessments. Tensile stability is also investigated, and the film retains 85% of its original luminous intensity when stretched to approximately 1.2, 1.6 and 1.9 times its original length (Fig. 8e & f). Flexibility is a crucial factor for the film's applicability in AC applications. The CIE coordinates of PVA film with different ratios (1–4 mg mL−1) of PCQDs solutions under 365 nm UV light are displayed in Fig. 8g. The CIE coordinates show that with an increasing ratio of PCQDs, the emission colour shifts from pale green to intense green. The results clearly show that the emission colour of the fabricated PCQDs films closely resembles that of PCQDs solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of latent fingerprints using PCQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The type of surface on which the LFPs are deposited may have an impact on the visualization efficacy of the LFPs, which in turn may have an effect on the overall effectiveness of the detection method. To evaluate the potential of PCQDs as LFP imaging agents, a variety of surfaces with differing properties, including smooth, semi-porous, non-porous, and porous surfaces, were examined as substrates [106]. These surfaces namely paper (porous), OHP sheet (semi-porous) and steel lock (non-porous) surfaces, respectively ( Fig. 9a-c). As evident from Fig. 9a-c, distinct FP patterns were successfully visualized on all substrates considered, showing good selectivity, good contrast and minimal background interference. Notably, paper exhibited the highest level of detailed information compared to the other substrates tested. These surfaces were selected as model surfaces for testing due to their frequent occurrence in everyday life and crime scenes. Fingerprint structures are typically categorized into three levels: level I (whorl, loop, and delta), level II (ridge bifurcations, ridge terminations, lakes, and crossovers), and level III (sweat-pores and scars) [107–109]. In Fig. 9d-f, model LFP patterns created on paper, OHP (overhead projector transparency sheet), and a steel lock using PCQDs are depicted. The images reveal visible and well-defined papillary ridge details, showcasing the sensitivity of the developer. Additionally, with little interference from the background, these pictures show an extreme contrast between the ridge pattern and the substrate background. A well-resolved ridge pattern structure, comprising level II elements like bifurcations, islands, ridge ends, and crossovers, among others, is displayed in the images, which show the flow of coloured ridges and colourless furrows. Furthermore, level 3 information, such as sweat pores and scars, is visible in these extracted images. Collectively, these details aid in distinguishing the unique characteristics of the fingerprint under examination and facilitate its identification.

To assess the quality of the developed LFPs, grayscale value (G) analysis was conducted using ImageJ software [110]. This semi-quantitative analysis helps distinguish between ridges and furrows based on grayscale values. The G/G0 parameter evaluates image resolution as shown in Fig. 9g-i, while the G0 parameter indicates the lowest grayscale value. Grayscale values fluctuate within the region shown by the red line in Fig. 9a-c. The G/G0 ratio in this instance is 32, emphasizing the importance of the ridge-to-furrow difference. The fact that the grayscale value for ridge parts is noticeably higher than for furrow sections lends more credence to this conclusion. The even dispersion of PCQDs across the surface of the developed LFPs is visually represented through 3D interactive plots (Fig. 9j-l).

A number of important factors need to be taken into account while analysing for personal identification namely, number, position (center or periphery of a ridge), inter-spacing (pore to pore distance), area (small/large) and shape (oval, circle, elliptical, rectangular, rhomboid) There are two ways to calculate the number of pores: calculating the average number of pores per unit length and unit area. Fig. 10a shows how many pores were identified using powder dusting with PCQDs in the developed images. In this instance, there are 8 pores on average for every friction ridge unit length. In contrast to the findings of Locard and O′Leary et al. [111], who reported 9 – 20 friction ridges per cm of friction ridge, Bindra et al. [112] previous study revealed 8 – 25 pores per cm of the epidermal ridge. The number of pores per unit length for each scenario is displayed in Table 4. Interestingly, there was a small but statistically significant difference in the laterality between the left and right-hand fingers. The characteristic that sets pores apart is that they are closed in the middle but open on one or both sides toward the perimeter. They may be positioned at the outside of the papillary ridge or in its middle. It is critical to understand that PCQDs adhesion to the pore edge determines whether a sweat pore is open or closed. The uniformity of PCQDs, the FP development process, and the pressure and angle used during FP deposition can all affect the pore nature and determine whether it is open on one or both sides or closed. Therefore, it is not expected that a pore taken from the same finger of the same person will have the same properties in this regard. The pore positions on the ridges of the formed FPs on glass, as seen in Fig. 10a and detailed in Table 4, indicate that closed pores are more common than open pores on one or both sides. The central position of the thumb and ring finger and the peripheral position of the index and ring finger have been found to have significant connections with the number of pores, as reported by Oklevski [113].

There are many different shapes and sizes of pores, such as oval, circle, square, triangular, rhomboid, elliptical. It is crucial to remember that pore shape is not a constant attribute because pressure distortions have the ability to change their fundamental form. A review of the various pore shapes that were found in produced FPs on glass is given in Table 2 . Fig. 10b illustrates these different pore shapes. While earlier study [114] revealed that the most common type of pores are rhomboid-shaped, more current studies show that elliptical/oval and circular/round pores are more common than rectangular and rhomboid pores. All of the experimental conditions used in this study observations revealed the presence of oval, round, square, and triangle pores. Furthermore, the inter-pore distance remained impressively constant during flexion despite pressure distortions and substantial deviations in the ridge path. Because the sweat pore centers on the same ridge are interconnected, each person's pattern is unique. A distinct pattern is produced by joining the centers of two nearby ridge pore centers. The developed FP pore inter-spacing on a glass surface is shown in Fig. 10c. Table 4 presents the results for the successive pore-to-pore distance of 10 pores, which are consistent with earlier research [115–117].

Fig. 10c and Table 4 display the diameter of the pores in FPs on glass along with the estimated values that correspond with it. The male and female donors had pore sizes between 89 and 103 µm and 76 and 82 µm, respectively. The results show that pore diameters can vary within a ridge without necessarily following a predetermined order. Large pores were less common than medium sized ones, which were followed by small-sized pores. Pore sizes were found to range from 88 to 220 µm and 88–200 µm, respectively, based on results published in Locard and Roddy and Stosz et al. [118]. In addition, new studies have revealed that the pore size ranges for males and females are 66–287 µm and 69–284 µm, respectively. Furthermore, by utilizing hydrochronic sensors and FL in polyvinyl pyrrolidone (PVP) composite film to assess variations in sweat pores secretory activity, it is possible to determine that pore size is dependent on physiological activity and is not a reliable method of matching two FPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A Python program for fingerprint image improvement, thinning, and minute extraction analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
Python software is also employed to delve into the intricate ridge features within both single and overlapped complex fingerprint images.
Figs. 11a and 12a exhibit single and overlapped fingerprints visualized under white light and 254 nm UV light on stainless steel and aluminium foil surfaces, respectively. The enhancement of fingerprint images involves segmentation and orientation estimation processes. For more in-depth insights into efficient fingerprint enhancement, please refer to the sections on segmentation and orientation [119]. Each enhancement procedure aims to eliminate unrecoverable portions while enhancing the quality of the ridge structures in recoverable sections. The segmentation phase is aimed at distinguishing the FP region (foreground) from the background, as shown in Figs. 11b and 12b. In contrast to the monotonous pattern in the backdrop, the foreground design is oriented and striped. To do this, we have used a really simple strategy depending on the size of the local gradient. To reduce background noise, we partitioned the fingerprints using a simple technique that considers the magnitude of the local gradient, the gradient magnitude integral, and a fundamental threshold. Figs. 11c and 12c show the fingerprint images integral gradient magnitude. The fingerprint images are segmented using a threshold of 255 (Figs. 11d and 12d). When tested on actual fingerprint photos with varying threshold values, picture segmentation with a variance threshold of 255 yielded the best results, as seen in Figs. 11d and 12d . In order to assess the fingerprint images orientation field, the orientation estimate step is essential. The gradient orientation is considered to be orthogonal to the projected ridge orientation when it is normalized within a window [120].

In these equations, Gx represents the local orientation of the gradient along the x-axis, Gy represents the local orientation of the gradient along the y-axis, and θ represents the angle of the ridge-valley orientation [121]. These equations are fundamental to understanding the outcomes of the Python program after compilation. Figs. 11e and 12e demonstrate the orientation field's results, which vividly display the rotation of ridges and furrows while being susceptible to noise and corrupted ridge and furrow structures. Fig. 11e, for instance, clearly illustrates the right loop's orientation. The ridge frequency, as well as level 1 and level 2 characteristics, will be described briefly in the following section. Estimating a local ridge frequency is crucial to improving the image's orientation. To do so, we selected specific regions for single prints and different regions for overlapped fingerprints, as seen in Figs. 11f and 12f. A sinusoidal pixel-to-pixel intensity change is seen in the projected waveform shown in Fig. 11g & 12g. The grayscale pixel profiles of well-formed fingerprints demonstrate that the material is correctly deposited on the ridges, not the furrows, as discussed in the previous section. This supports the earlier finding that ridge areas have considerably higher grayscale values than furrow areas. We used a bank of Gabor filters in a contextual convolution to improve the FP pattern [122]. The results of applying a Gabor filter to FP images are shown in Figs. 11h and 12h. The image is thinned and binarized to improve its suitability for feature extraction after the Gabor filtering step. Figs. 11i-j and 12i-j show the binarized and thinned images. Finding the threshold (T) to reduce cluster overlap and maximize the size of each cluster is the binarization methods objective [123]. Experiments for thinning and binarization are shown in Figs. 11k and 12k. Visual analysis of the outcomes demonstrates that the background (black pixels) and the ridges from the valleys (white pixels) are successfully separated by the binarization process. After binarization and thinning, the next step is to extract precise small features. These tiny features can be bifurcations or terminations, and they play a crucial role in the alignment and matching processes. All potential minute points are shown inFigs. 11m-o and 12m-o, where termination points are shown in blue and bifurcation points are shown in red. Following the thinning and binarization of the images, Figs. 11p and 12p clearly demonstrate the level-I and level-II features (1–14 in Fig. 11p for single fingerprint images and 1–10 in Fig. 12p for overlapped fingerprint images). Prominent level-I features include the delta and core, while level-II elements such as islands, lakes, bridges, short hooks, long hooks, enclosures, dots, bifurcations, and trifurcations are clearly visible in both single and overlapped images [124].

The PCQDs demonstrated noteworthy antioxidant activity using the DPPH method, although to a slightly lesser extent than ascorbic acid, a positive control ( Fig. 13a). As the concentration of PCQDs increased, so did its DPPH scavenging activity. The PCQDs exhibited a maximum potential of 85.42% in reducing DPPH free radicals, while ascorbic acid achieved a higher level at 98.76%. The IC50 values for scavenging were 102.37 ± 0.38 for PCQDs and 52.194 ± 1.59 for ascorbic acid. When PCQDs were added to the violet DPPH solution, it caused a colour change to a light yellowish hue that intensified with higher concentrations. This change may be attributed to the pairing of the unpaired electron of the DPPH radical with cations or H+ groups, which could be associated with PCQDs extract adsorbed on the surface of the PCQDs, acting as a capping agent or residue [125].

We conducted an in-vitro study to evaluate the impact of PCQDs on cell viability. The cytotoxic potential of PCQDs on HTC-116 cells is illustrated in Fig. 13b & c. When HCT-116 cells were exposed to PCQDs, a considerable degree of cytotoxicity was observed, resulting in an IC50 value of 57.43 µg mL−1. Furthermore, at PCQDs concentrations of 50, 100, and 200 μg mL−1, clear morphological changes were evident in HTC-116 cells. These changes included cell detachment, membrane blebbing, and distorted cell shapes, indicating a significant reduction in cellular viability (Fig. 13c).

To assess the cellular uptake efficiency of PCQDs by HTC116 cells, we conducted flow cytometry analysis of HCT 116 cells after a 6-hour incubation ( Fig. 14a-e). After treating HCT 116 cells with 57.43 μg mL−1 of PCQDs for 6 hrs, the expression of FITC in HCT-116 cells significantly increased, similar to the effect of 5 fluorouracil (used as a positive control) at 50 µM mL−1, thus confirming substantial cellular uptake in HCT-116 cells. We also determined the percentage of cells associated with PCQDs and 5 fluorouracil, and the results revealed that after 6 hrs of incubation, 64.05% of cells were associated with PCQDs, and 82.84% were associated with 5-fluorouracil.

We conducted an apoptosis study through flow cytometric analysis using Annexin V/PI staining, which revealed a substantial reduction in HCT 116 cell survival (Fig. 14f-j). The PCQDs significantly increased apoptosis to 47.54% and induced 20.03% necrosis in HCT 116 cells. PCQDs demonstrated a similar apoptotic potential as 5-fluorouracil (used as the positive control) with a high percentage of apoptosis (71.47%), while only 1.17% of cells exhibited early and late apoptosis in untreated cells. Based on the early and late apoptotic data, we concluded that 24 hrs of PCQDs treatment specifically induced apoptosis, with a majority of the cell population shifting toward early apoptosis compared to untreated HCT-116 cells. The HTC 116 cells were treated with DMSO (negative control), 50 µM of 5-fluorouracil (positive control), and PCQDs at 57.43 µg mL−1 for 24 hrs. In this study, cells treated with 5 fluorouracil significantly increased DCF expression in HCT-116 cells, and PCQDs effectively enhanced DCF expression, confirming that PCQDs induce apoptosis through cellular oxidative stress in human colon cancer cells ( Fig. 15a-e).

The antimicrobial activity of the synthesized PCQDs is presented in Fig. 15. Staphylococcus aureus (MTCC 1430), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (MTCC 424), and Candida albicans (MTCC 227) were cultured on Brain Heart Infusion PCQDs and Luria Bertani PCQDs, respectively (Fig. 15 f-h). They were treated with varying concentrations of PCQDs (10, 25, and 50 µg mL−1) and a positive control (ampicillin for MTCC 1430 and MTCC 424; amphotericin for MTCC 227). Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, when treated with PCQDs and ampicillin, exhibited significant inhibition zones of 6 mm and 13 mm, respectively at a concentration of 50 µg mL−1 PCQDs. The positive control (ampicillin) resulted in inhibition zones of 17 mm and 11 mm for Staphylococcus aureus and Pseudomonas aeruginosa, respectively (Fig. 15f-f1 and g-g1). Candida albicans, when treated with PCQDs and amphotericin, displayed a significant inhibition of 10 mm and 2 mm, respectively (Fig. 15h & h1).

The plausible mechanism of antibacterial and anticancer activity for the prepared PCQDs is shown in Fig. 16 . The decorative oxygen containing groups are responsible for the mortal effects of the prepared PCQDs against the tested microbial cells through the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS). The liberated ROS acted in killing the microbial cells, as ROS were adhered then penetrated the microbial cell wall to act in motivating of the oxidative stress with deteriorating DNA & RNA, leading to inhibit and alter the gene expressions. Additionally, ROS act in the dys-function of mitochondria, lipid per-oxidization, inactivating of intracellular protein, gradual degradation of the cell wall, and eventually leaded to apoptotic cell death. PCQDs-HT showed significantly enhanced microbicide potentiality attributing to the effectiveness of hydrothermal conditions in nucleation of small and size controllable PCQDs to be easily penetrated to the microbial cell wall for eventual cell mortality [126,127].

The antibacterial mechanism followed by PCQDs is a complex and different process from that of conventional antibiotics and it includes ROS generation, degeneration of cell structure, and leakage of the cytoplasm as a result of DNA binding and modulation of gene expression [128]. The electrostatic attraction of PCQDs to the microbial cell is extremely reliant on the surface charge state of PCQDs. The type of bacterial strains, the intrinsic properties of CDs, and their surface modification also influence the overall bactericidal performance. The aforementioned factors that influence the bactericidal efficiency of PCQDs are be explained using photoinduced antibacterial properties. By photoexcitation, electron-hole pairs form in the carbon core of PCQDs and are localized at the surface defects. The recombination of these electron-hole pairs causes fluorescence emissions. In addition, generation of electron-hole pairs and their transfer to the associated proximal species can produce radical species (OH and O2−), which can impose oxidative damages on bacterial cells, i.e., morphological changes of the cell membrane, cytoplasmic leakage, and DNA impairment. Walia et al. [129] synthesized PCQDs that emit bright blue and red light. The derived PCQDs could eradicate E. coli and S. aureus by oxidative stress-induced DNA degradation. For different concentrations, the obtained PCQDs found to be more effective against E. coli. GQDs synthesized by Sun et al. [130] exhibited strong antibacterial properties against both S. aureus and E. coli bacteria. PCQDs with their excellent biocompatibility adhere to the bacterial membrane and decompose H2O2 into highly antibacterial -OH and cause cell damage. Wan et al. [131] synthesized antibiotic-based PDs (Vancomycin-PDs and Polymyxin B-PDs) with photo-killing capability against S. aureus and P. aeruginosa.

In summary, we successfully synthesized green-emitting PCQDs via a straightforward, eco-friendly, and sustainable hydrothermal method, using pistachio shells as the only source of carbon, eliminating the requirement for organic reagents or additives. The water solubility and excitation-dependent fluorescence characteristics of these water-soluble PCQDs were impressive. Especially, they showed a long lifetime, good FL stability, and a high QY of about 15%. We harnessed the exceptional properties of PCQDs for applications like fluorescent ink and sensitive Cu2+ detection. These PCQDs displayed remarkable sensitivity and selectivity for Cu2+ with a broad linear relationship (R2 = 0.9847) and an impressively low limit of detection (LoD) at 0.351 µM within a concentration range of 1 – 4 μM, attributed to dynamic quenching mechanisms. Furthermore, we harnessed these PCQDs to construct NOT and IMPLICATION logic gates, capitalizing on their FL turn ON-OFF behaviour when exposed to Cd2+. Additionally, we utilized them to create a fluorescent ink for writing and pattern formation, enabling secure encryption and decryption operations. Expanding their utility, we incorporated these PCQDs, derived from an abundant and non-toxic source, into flexible and transparent PVA films. This novel approach resulted in highly transparent films with enhanced UV blocking and UV-absorption properties. These PCQDs represent a cost-effective and reliable pathway for various applications, showcasing their versatility. In addition, we explored their utility in personal identification through the powder dusting method, providing level III features for analysing LFPs on various surfaces without causing any damage. The readily available and benign nature of the materials used further highlights their potential for a wide array of applications.
